PasswordReset: A reset workflow that issues a one-shot, time-bounded token requires three orthogonal pieces of information that no single status field can encode: where in the lifecycle the request currently sits, when the token was minted relative to the clock, and whether the token has already been spent. The status enum captures the lifecycle, issued_at fixes the start of the validity window, and the token_used boolean is what actually prevents replay across a Reset that re-enters the issued state. The clock variable now is bounded by MaxTime so the model has finitely many configurations, and Tick is the only action that advances it, which keeps temporal reasoning local. The UseToken guard now <= issued_at + 1 is what makes the unexpired-at-consumption property hold; Expire is the dual edge that fires once the window closes. The history flag ever_issued exists so the safety property can talk about the past without weakening the invariant: a reset_done state must be reachable only through a real issuance, and that implication is what the invariant pins down.
PaxosSingleDecree: Single-decree Paxos is hard precisely because the safety obligation is global -- at most one value is ever chosen across all ballots and all quorums -- yet every protocol step is local to one acceptor. The abstraction here collapses the entire protocol state into a growing set of message records plus a single chosen variable, which is the right move because Paxos is monotone: messages are never retracted, so the message set is the natural carrier of history. The interesting guard is inside SendAccept, where the conjunct that every prior accept message already in the set must agree on v abstracts away the standard pick-the-highest-prior-promise rule into a single global predicate. That predicate is exactly the inductive heart of the safety proof, because once any quorum can witness an accepted value, the guard prevents any acceptor from later accepting a different one. Learn fires only when a quorum of accepts for the same (b,v) is visible, and Reset wipes the message set after a decision so the bounded ballot range and acceptor set keep the reachable space finite.
PaymentStateMachine: Payment processing looks like a plain enum walk, but the safety obligations cross time: a refund must witness a prior capture, and a void must witness the absence of one. A status field alone cannot answer these questions because the moment status leaves captured for refunded, the evidence of capture is gone. The fix is three monotone history flags -- ever_authorized, ever_captured, ever_voided -- each set exactly once at the action that names it and never cleared. With those flags in place the safety predicate becomes a conjunction of three implications that read directly off the current status, and it is inductive because the only actions that can change status are the ones that also raise the corresponding flag in the same step. Capture and Void share the authorized predecessor but are mutually exclusive futures; the absence of any edge from voided guarantees that ever_captured stays false on every voided trajectory, which is exactly what the second clause of the invariant needs.
PerCpuCache:
Slab caches exist to amortize global-pool synchronization, so the modeling unit has to be the *count* of objects in each location, not the objects themselves -- the spec elides identity entirely and tracks only how many sit in each CPU-local cache, in the global pool, and in client hands. Conservation is the load-bearing property: TotalCached + pool + inuse = TotalObjects must hold across every action. Refill and Drain are the only edges that move objects between local caches and the pool, and they do so in fixed Batch quanta, which is what real slab allocators do; Alloc and Free shuttle a single object between a cache and the inuse counter without touching the pool. Each of those four actions adds and subtracts the same magnitude on opposite sides of the conservation equation, which makes the invariant inductive by inspection. CacheBounded keeps each per-CPU count inside 0..CacheCap, and because Refill only fires when the local cache is empty and Drain only when it is full, those bounds are never violated even though Batch is smaller than CacheCap.
The recursive sum TotalCached over the finite Cpus set keeps the conservation predicate fully evaluable as a closed-form expression.
PetersonMutex: Peterson's algorithm is the textbook example of a mutual-exclusion protocol that uses only single-writer shared variables and no atomic test-and-set, so the spec must faithfully represent the three preparatory steps a process performs before reading the competing flag. The pc function tracks each process through ncs, set, wait, cs; the per-process flag announces intent; and the single shared turn carries the courtesy variable that breaks symmetric races. The subtle point is the order: SetFlag raises my intent before SetTurn yields the turn, because if those two were swapped a process could find itself in cs while the other still believed the turn was theirs. The Enter guard flag[Other(i)] = FALSE \/ turn = i is the entire safety argument -- if both processes are in wait, exactly one of them holds the turn, and the other's flag is necessarily true, so only the turn-holder's disjunct can fire. Leave clears the flag so the partner's wait can resolve, which preserves progress without weakening safety. The mutual-exclusion clause folded into TypeOK forbids any reachable state with both pc[i] and pc[j] equal to cs.
Pipeline: A bounded three-stage pipeline is a conservation problem dressed up as a dataflow problem. The natural state is the occupancy of each stage's queue plus two history counters that record the cumulative number of items that have entered stage one and left stage three; the counters exist solely so the conservation equation in_count = q1 + q2 + q3 + out_count can be stated as a closed-form invariant rather than a temporal one. Each Move action subtracts one from the upstream queue and adds one to the downstream queue in the same step, so it is conservation-neutral; Ingest raises in_count and q1 together; Emit drops q3 and raises out_count together. The QueuesBounded clause is enforced action-locally because every move action that increments a queue checks that queue's capacity K beforehand, and Ingest checks q1 < K. Drain is a reset edge that fires only when the pipeline has fully flushed and the input quota is exhausted, which keeps the reachable space finite under unbounded ingestion semantics.
PriorityCeilingMutex: The priority ceiling protocol is interesting because the safety obligation is not just mutual exclusion -- it is a priority discipline that bounds inversion to a single critical section. Modeling it requires three pieces: where each task is in its lifecycle (pc), who currently owns the resource (holder), and what *effective* priority each task is running at right now. The effective priority must be a separate variable from the base priority because Acquire mutates it: when a task takes the lock its effective priority is raised to Ceiling, and Release restores it to BasePrio[t]. That update is the entire mechanism by which a low-priority holder cannot be preempted by an intermediate-priority task that might later request the same lock. The invariants folded into TypeOK encode three structural facts: a non-none holder is the unique task in cs, that holder runs at the ceiling, and no two tasks are simultaneously in cs. The last clause is the mutual-exclusion guarantee, and it is preserved because Acquire requires holder = NoHolder and Release sets it back to NoHolder atomically.
PriorityQueue: Modeling a multilevel priority queue is mostly about deciding what *not* to track. Items themselves carry no identity beyond their priority class, so the entire data structure collapses into a function counts: Prios -> Nat that records how many items currently sit at each level. Push at level p increments counts[p] subject to the per-level cap K, which is what keeps the state space finite. The interesting move is in Pop, where the priority discipline is encoded by HighestNonEmpty, a CHOOSE that picks the unique p whose count is positive and for which every strictly higher priority is empty. That definition means a Pop step decrements the highest occupied level by construction, so the safety property -- a pop never returns a lower priority while a higher exists -- is enforced by the Pop action's structure rather than by an external invariant. Bounded then needs only to assert per-level membership in 0..K, which is preserved because Push checks the cap before incrementing and Pop only fires when some level is positive.
PriorityScheduler: A preemptive priority scheduler differs from a plain run-queue because new arrivals can dethrone the currently running task, so the spec must model both the scheduling decision and the preemption boundary. The state has two components: a single running slot (with sentinel NoTask for idle) and a ready set; together they partition the live tasks at any instant. Dispatch chooses a ready task whose priority equals MaxReadyPrio, which makes the dispatcher non-deterministic among ties but never wrong about ordering. The Arrive action splits into three disjuncts depending on whether the CPU is idle, the arrival is no higher than the running task (it joins ready), or strictly higher (the running task is bumped back into ready and the arrival takes its place). That third disjunct is the only place a non-NoTask running can be displaced without completing, and it is gated on Prio(t) > Prio(running), which is exactly what makes PriorityInv inductive: any state where a higher-priority task sits in ready while a lower-priority one runs is unreachable, because the only way to put running back into ready is through that very preemption guard.
ProducerConsumer: The bounded buffer is the canonical concurrent abstraction, but the right way to spec it for safety is to forget about the items themselves and reason about three counters: the current occupancy, the cumulative number produced, and the cumulative number consumed. Production is gated by buffer < K so the buffer never overflows; consumption is gated by buffer > 0 so it never underflows. The conservation law that ties the three together is produced - consumed = buffer, which holds at Init (all zeros) and is preserved by every action because Produce raises buffer and produced together while Consume lowers buffer and raises consumed by the same amount. Reset is the bookkeeping edge that allows the model to keep exploring once the produced quota MaxItems is exhausted; it only fires when the buffer is empty and consumed has caught up, which means it leaves the conservation equation intact at zero. The safety predicate PCSafe pins all three of these facts together so they form a single inductive invariant.
PromiseFuture: A future is a single-assignment cell, which means the safety obligation is monotonicity: once state transitions from Empty to Fulfilled it stays there forever, and the value field is only meaningful in the post-transition phase. The spec captures this with a tiny two-valued state plus a value field that the invariant correlates with state -- value = 0 exactly when empty, value in 1..MaxVal exactly when fulfilled. Fulfill is guarded by state = Empty so it cannot fire twice, which is the entire single-assignment guarantee. The readers set is what makes the spec interesting: it tracks which consumers have already observed the value, and Read is guarded by state = Fulfilled so a consumer can never witness the cell in the empty phase. ResetReaders is a finiteness escape hatch -- it fires only when every consumer has read, and clearing the set lets the spec keep exploring without ever revisiting Empty for state, so monotonicity of the assignment is preserved across the cycle even though the readership churns.
QueuedRequests: FIFO commit ordering is the kind of property that begs for a history variable, and that is exactly what enq is here -- it accumulates the full enqueue sequence and never shrinks, while queue holds the live tail and committed accumulates the prefix that has fired. The clever part is the structural invariant: Len(committed) + Len(queue) = Len(enq), with committed equal to the first Len(committed) elements of enq and queue equal to the remaining suffix. That decomposition is what makes the FIFO discipline an *equational* invariant rather than a temporal one. Enqueue is the only edge that grows enq, and it does so by appending the same fresh id (Len(enq)+1) to both enq and queue, which keeps queue aligned with enq's tail. Commit moves Head(queue) to the end of committed and tails the queue, which is exactly the operation that preserves the prefix-plus-suffix decomposition. The fresh id scheme guarantees no duplicates so the indexing never aliases.
QuorumRead: Quorum reads with read-repair turn a read into a write, which makes the spec interesting because the only state variable is the per-replica version map ver. Both Write and ReadRepair are quorum-quantified actions: each picks an arbitrary subset Q whose cardinality meets the majority threshold. Write also picks a fresh version v that is strictly greater than every replica in Q currently holds, then bumps every replica in Q up to v -- this is the source of monotonicity. ReadRepair computes the maximum version visible across its quorum and writes it back to every member of the quorum, which never decreases any replica and brings stragglers up to the witnessed maximum. The intersection clause (any two majorities share a replica) is folded into the invariant as a structural fact about subsets of Replicas; combined with version monotonicity it implies that any subsequent quorum will witness at least the latest written version, which is the entire safety story for read-after-write.
QuorumWrite: Quorum-write replication needs three pieces of state: the per-replica accepted value, a global chosen marker, and the implicit set of valid quorums derived from the threshold W. The store maps each replica to either a value or none; chosen records whether a value has been committed. The crucial guard lives inside Accept: a replica can take a write of v only if every replica currently holds either none or that same v, which serializes proposals into a single agreed-upon value before any commit can fire. Commit promotes a value to chosen once some quorum holds it uniformly, and Reset wipes the store so the next round can begin. The safety invariant says that whenever chosen is non-none, every quorum contains at least one replica that holds the chosen value -- this follows from the intersection property of majorities together with the fact that some quorum was already uniform on chosen at commit time. The strict Accept guard is what makes the inductive step work; weakening it would let two values race past the threshold simultaneously.
Quota: Per-tenant quota enforcement is the smallest interesting concurrency spec: a single function inflight from tenants to natural numbers, plus two actions that increment and decrement on a per-tenant basis. The Start guard inflight[t] < C is the entire enforcement mechanism, and it is action-local in the strongest possible sense -- the only way inflight[t] can grow is through Start(t), and Start(t) cannot fire when the count already equals C, so the upper bound is preserved by inspection. Finish is symmetric and guarded against underflow. There is no cross-tenant coupling at all, which is why the invariant decomposes cleanly into a universal quantifier over Tenants. The smallness of N (assumed in 1..3) and C (= 2) keeps the reachable space tiny, but the inductive structure is independent of those bounds and would scale to any finite tenant set.
RWBakery: The reader-writer bakery generalizes Lamport's original by letting each contender pick a role in addition to a ticket, which means the entry guards must distinguish two asymmetric exclusion regimes -- writers exclude everyone, readers exclude only writers with smaller tickets. Three per-process arrays carry exactly the information needed: pc for the protocol phase, role for the chosen role, and number for the bakery ticket. Pick draws a ticket equal to one plus the maximum of the others, mirroring the door function in the original; capping it at MaxNum is what bounds the state. EnterReader's guard scans only writers and demands lexicographic priority over them via LessEq, which lets multiple readers cohabit in cs. EnterWriter's guard is stricter: no other process may be in cs at all, and any waiting peer must have a strictly larger (ticket, id). The combined effect is that the only way two processes can simultaneously be in cs is if both are readers, which is exactly the safety property folded into the type invariant.
RaftElection: Raft's election safety is a per-term property -- at most one leader exists for any given term -- so a leader function indexed by Terms is the right state shape rather than a single global slot. Each server tracks its own state, currentTerm, votedFor (the candidate it backed in its current term), and votesGranted (the set of voters that have promised it). StartElection bumps the candidate's term and self-votes; GrantVote transfers a vote from a follower to a candidate, but only if the follower has not yet voted at that term, which is the at-most-one-vote-per-term rule that makes the safety argument go through. BecomeLeader fires once votesGranted is a majority and the leader slot for the term is still vacant; that vacancy check, combined with the fact that any two majorities intersect on at least one server which can vote at most once per term, prevents two leaders from co-existing in the same term. Reset is the round restart that keeps the model finite. The election-safety predicate is the explicit contract that two leaders sharing a term must be the same server.
RateLimiter: The token bucket has a deceptively simple specification: a single integer tokens that lives in 0..K, plus an in-flight admitted counter that records requests issued but not yet drained. Refill bumps tokens up to K, modeling the fixed refill rate as a non-deterministic event that respects the cap. Admit consumes one token and raises admitted, which is gated both by tokens > 0 (the rate-limiting condition) and by admitted < K (a finiteness fence so the model space stays bounded). Deliver is the dual edge that retires an in-flight request without touching the token bucket. The two-clause invariant tokens in 0..K /\ admitted in 0..K is preserved by inspection of each action because every increment is paired with the corresponding bound check, and every decrement is paired with the corresponding positivity check. The capacity-K admitted bound encodes the per-window safety promise that no burst can exceed bucket plus accumulated refill within any sliding observation.
ReadWriteLock: Two sets carry the entire state: readers, the (possibly empty) collection of processes currently holding the shared lock, and writer, a set of cardinality zero or one used as an option type rather than as a collection. Modeling writer as a set instead of a process-or-none scalar lets the safety predicate reason about emptiness with set algebra rather than sentinel comparisons. AcquireRead is gated on writer = {}, so a reader can only join when no writer is present; AcquireWrite is gated on both readers = {} and writer = {}, which enforces full exclusion at acquisition time. Once a writer holds the lock no further reader or writer can enter, because both Acquire actions test writer = {}. The RWSafe invariant is the conjunction of three structural facts -- at least one of writer or readers is empty, writer's cardinality is at most one, and the two sets are disjoint -- which together enforce the standard mutual-exclusion regime and are preserved because every action either adds to one side while the other is verified empty or removes from a side without touching the other.
ReadWriteLockTxn: Two-phase locking on a single resource has a four-cell compatibility matrix that collapses to three rules: shared locks compose with each other, exclusive locks compose with nothing, and a transaction may not double-hold. Modeling this as two sets sLocks and xLocks rather than a single per-transaction state lets the safety predicate be a structural disjunction over set emptiness instead of a per-pair quantification. AcquireS demands xLocks = {} so a writer never coexists with a reader; AcquireX additionally demands sLocks = {} so a writer never coexists with any reader, and because xLocks must already be empty no two writers can hold simultaneously. Both acquire actions check that the requesting transaction does not already hold the other lock, which is what the disjointness clause sLocks ∩ xLocks = {} pins down. The cardinality bound on xLocks reduces the writer-uniqueness obligation to set arithmetic, and ReleaseX wipes the entire writer set in one step because there is at most one element to remove.
RecursiveMutex: A recursive mutex needs to remember two things that a plain mutex does not: who currently holds the lock and how many times that holder has acquired it without yet releasing. The pair (holder, depth) is the smallest representation that supports both questions, with the additional invariant (holder = NoHolder) <=> (depth = 0) tying the two together so neither can drift independently. AcquireFresh fires only when holder is NoHolder and seeds depth at 1; AcquireRecursive fires only when the current process is already the holder, so the rule that only the holder may re-enter is enforced action-locally. ReleaseOne and ReleaseFinal are split because the depth-going-to-zero case must also clear the holder, while ReleaseOne strictly above 1 only decrements the counter -- both are guarded on holder = i, which encodes the only-the-holder-may-decrement rule. The mutual-exclusion clause in TypeOK forbids two distinct processes from sitting in cs simultaneously, and that holds because AcquireFresh requires the lock to be free and recursive entries cannot change which process is in cs.
ReentrantMutex: Where the recursive-mutex spec uses a sentinel-bearing scalar for the holder, this reentrant variant uses a set-of-at-most-one for owner so that emptiness rather than equality with a placeholder encodes the free state. The depth counter and the owner set must move in lockstep: the invariant (owner = {}) <=> (depth = 0) is what prevents a degenerate state where depth is positive but no one owns the lock or vice versa. Acquire creates the singleton from empty and seeds depth at 1; Reenter is gated on owner = {p}, which is the only place a non-owner is rejected, encoding the safety property that only the holder can deepen the recursion. ReleasePartial decrements without clearing owner, while ReleaseFinal is the unique edge that empties the singleton, and it requires depth = 1 so the counter and the owner set transition to the free state in the same step. The cardinality bound on owner closes the loop: at most one process ever holds the lock at any point in any reachable state.
RefCountGc: Reference counting is interesting to model because the safety obligation is negative: live objects must never be freed. The state needs two per-object pieces of information -- the current count rc[o] and a sticky freed[o] flag -- and the relation between them is the entire correctness contract. Inc and Dec are guarded on ~freed[o], so a freed object can never resurrect; Dec additionally has an asymmetric floor for the Root object that prevents its count from dropping below 1, which is what makes RootNeverFreed inductive. Free fires only when rc[o] = 0, and it never modifies rc, so once an object is freed its count remains pinned at zero -- the FreedHasZeroRc invariant follows directly from this combined with the Inc guard. The Root object's invariant lower bound of 1 plus the Free guard rc = 0 together mean that Root can never satisfy Free's precondition, which closes the proof of the no-live-object-freed property. Idle is the finiteness escape that fires once everything reachable has been collected.
RefundFlow: Partial refunds present a budget-tracking problem on top of a state machine: the spec needs the lifecycle phase plus a running tally of how much has been refunded so far, since the partial-refund action must check refunded_total against Original on every fire. The status enum walks pending -> captured -> partially_refunded -> fully_refunded with the final transition triggered automatically inside PartialRefund whenever the new total reaches Original. The history flag ever_captured exists so the safety invariant can encode the temporal property that any refund-flavored status implies a prior capture occurred -- even though status alone retains that information here, the flag makes the invariant local and inductive without forward-walking the state graph. The bound refunded_total <= Original is preserved by the PartialRefund guard refunded_total < Original, and the implication that refunded_total > 0 entails ever_captured is preserved because refunded_total can only grow inside PartialRefund, which requires status in {captured, partially_refunded}, both of which set ever_captured to TRUE earlier.
ReplicatedLog: Modeling a replicated append-only log without modeling the entries themselves works because the safety property is about *positions*, not contents: each replica is fully described by its current log length, and the global commit index records the highest position confirmed by a quorum. LeaderAppend extends one replica's logLen, Replicate copies a single position from a longer source to a shorter destination (so a follower's log can never overtake a leader's by skipping), and Commit raises committed to any n that a quorum of replicas already covers. The structural invariant -- the cardinality of replicas with logLen >= committed is at least Quorum -- is preserved because Commit only fires when that very condition holds at the chosen n, and neither LeaderAppend nor Replicate ever decreases any logLen. Two majorities always intersect on at least one replica, which means once a position is committed it is forever witnessed by some replica in any future quorum read.
ReservationSystem: Seat reservation is a per-seat decoupled problem, so the natural state is two boolean arrays indexed by seats: booked records the current state, ever_booked records whether the seat has ever been taken at all. The double-booking property -- at most one active reservation per seat -- is enforced by the Book action's guard ~booked[s], which simply refuses to fire on an already-booked seat; there is no need for cross-seat reasoning because the actions act independently per index. The history flag ever_booked is what allows the invariant booked[s] => ever_booked[s] to be inductive: every Book step that flips booked[s] to TRUE also raises ever_booked[s], and Cancel only clears booked, never ever_booked. The Done idle action triggers once every seat has been touched at least once, providing a stuttering sink that prevents deadlock without ever revisiting a violating state.
ResourceHierarchy: Lock ordering eliminates the circular-wait condition by demanding that resources be acquired in a fixed total order, and the spec encodes that discipline directly in the Acquire guard: process p can take resource r only if it already holds every resource numbered below r. The single state variable is a map holds : Resources -> Procs ∪ {NoOne}, which captures both per-resource ownership and (by inversion) which resources each process is currently holding. ReleaseTop is the only release edge, and it picks the highest-numbered resource currently held by p, which mirrors the reverse-order release rule and ensures that a process never has a hole in its held prefix. The OrderingInv invariant says exactly that -- if p holds r then p holds every lower-numbered resource -- and it is inductive because Acquire only adds the next-higher resource to a contiguous prefix and ReleaseTop only removes the topmost. Per-resource mutual exclusion is automatic since holds[r] is a scalar, and the prefix-discipline rules out any deadlock cycle by induction on the order.
ResourceLease: A lease is a mutex with a deadline, so two pieces of information must travel together: which process currently holds the resource, and how many ticks have elapsed since the holder acquired it. The pair (holder, ticks) is the smallest sufficient state, and the invariant (holder = NoOne) => (ticks = 0) ties them so the tick counter cannot drift away from the ownership when the resource is free. Acquire is gated on holder = NoOne and resets ticks to zero in the same step. Tick is the only edge that advances the counter, and it is bounded by ticks < Lease so the counter never overshoots. Expire fires precisely when ticks reaches the deadline, atomically clearing both the holder and the counter; Release is the voluntary equivalent for the strict-less-than-Lease case. Mutual exclusion is automatic because holder is a scalar that only Acquire can set away from NoOne, and the lease bound ticks <= Lease is preserved by inspection of every action.
RetryWithBackoff: Exponential backoff is interesting because the delay variable is constrained to a power-of-two ladder rather than a contiguous range, so the invariant has to assert delay in {1,2,4,8} as a discrete membership rather than a numeric bound. The doubling rule lives in FailAndBackoff, which sets the next delay to delay * 2 unless that would exceed MaxDelay, in which case it caps at MaxDelay -- this cap is what makes the power-of-two membership stable, because hitting 8 simply pins delay there for any further failures. The state has three components: attempts (the retry count), delay (the current backoff window), and status (running, succeeded, given_up). Succeed and GiveUp are the two terminal transitions out of running, with GiveUp gated on attempts = MaxAttempts - 1 so that the final attempt counter lands at exactly MaxAttempts. The PowOf2 predicate folded into TypeOK is preserved because every transition that touches delay either leaves it alone or doubles a power of two (or caps at MaxDelay, which is itself a power of two).
RingBuffer: A SPSC ring buffer can be modeled with three integers: head, tail, and an explicit size, even though size is technically derivable from head and tail under the modular arithmetic. Carrying size as a separate variable is what lets the spec distinguish full from empty -- both states have head = tail, and without an extra bit they are indistinguishable. The structural invariant ((tail - head) mod K) = (size mod K) ties the indices to the count and is preserved by both actions: Produce advances tail by one mod K and increments size, Consume advances head by one mod K and decrements size, so each side of the equation moves in lockstep. The Produce guard size < K is the full-buffer barrier and the Consume guard size > 0 is the empty-buffer barrier; together they ensure head and tail never collide except at exactly the empty or full state, which is the spec's correctness contract.
RingElection: Token-based ring election sends a single record around a unidirectional ring, accumulating the maximum id encountered along the way. The token is encoded as a record with three fields -- holder, maxSeen, and hops -- because all three pieces of information must move atomically as the token is forwarded; splitting them across separate variables would lose the per-step coupling. Pass advances holder to the successor and updates maxSeen to the larger of the new holder's id and the previous maxSeen, while incrementing the hops counter that bounds the ring traversal at N steps and keeps the model finite. Elect fires once hops has reached N-1 and maxSeen equals N, which is the largest possible id; this is the only edge that promotes a process to leader, and its guard guarantees that the elected leader is exactly the maximum-id process. Reset wipes the leader and reseeds a fresh token so the protocol can run again. The safety predicate leader # 0 => leader >= every j is preserved because Elect only fires when maxSeen has truly observed the maximum.
RiverCrossingFamily: The classic family river-crossing puzzle is interesting because the safety constraint is a per-bank semantic predicate, not a structural arithmetic one: a bank is unsafe iff the thief is present alongside any family member without the policeman to chaperone. The natural state is two sets left and right partitioning People, plus a boat-side scalar that serializes the crossings. SafeBank captures the constraint as a three-way disjunction over thief absence, police presence, or empty family -- and the spec applies it to *both* banks after every crossing, since the side that lost the boat is just as constrained as the side that gained it. ValidLoad enforces the seating rule that one or two passengers must include at least one adult driver. Crossings non-deterministically pick a subset S of the embarking bank, transfer it, and only commit if both resulting banks pass the safety predicate. The boat scalar alternates between L and R, ensuring that successive crossings come from alternating banks. The bank partition is preserved by every action because crossings only move people, never duplicate or destroy them.
RoundRobinScheduler: Round-robin scheduling on a single CPU has only two pieces of state: which task is currently running (with NoTask as a sentinel) and whose turn it is to run next. The crucial invariant is the rotation discipline -- whenever a task is running, it must be the task whose index equals turn -- which is captured by the disjunctive predicate (running = NoTask) \/ (running = turn). Schedule is the only edge that promotes a task from idle to running, and it copies turn directly into running, so the equality is established at every transition into the running phase. Preempt is the only edge that vacates the running slot, and in the same step it advances turn by one modulo N, which keeps the rotation order strictly cyclic. Because turn is only mutated by Preempt and only incremented (never reset), each task gets exactly one slot per cycle in the canonical 0..N-1 order. The fact that running is a scalar guarantees mutual exclusion -- at most one running task -- by construction.
RubiksFace: Modeling a single face of a Rubik's cube is really an exercise in characterizing rotations as permutations and then proving that the multiset of colors is invariant under those permutations. The state is a single function face from a 3x3 cell grid to colors. Each rotation is a closed-form expression on cell coordinates: Rot90 maps (r,c) to f[c, N+1-r], and Rot180 and Rot270 are the obvious analogs; these are total permutations of the cell domain, so the resulting face is just a relabeling of the same color values at different positions. Counting how many times each color appears -- CountColor -- is therefore preserved by every rotation, since permutations preserve cardinalities of preimages. The InitCount table fixes the multiset profile of the starting face, and SafetyInv asserts the per-color count is identical to that profile in every reachable state. The invariant is inductive in the most direct sense possible: every transition is a permutation of cells, and permutations conserve color cardinalities.
RushHour: The Rush Hour board can be specified compactly by representing each car only by its head cell rather than by every occupied square -- the footprint is reconstructed on demand from the head plus the car's known length and orientation. CellsA expands a length-2 horizontal car to two cells; CellsB expands a length-3 vertical car to three. The InBoard predicates encode the bounds: a horizontal car of length 2 has its head's column constrained to 1..N-1, and a vertical car of length 3 has its head's row constrained to 1..N-2, so any reachable head position guarantees the entire footprint stays on the board. The slide actions move a car by one cell along its axis and check both InBoard for the candidate head and NoOverlap with the other car; only if both hold does the new head replace the old one. The safety invariant is the conjunction of both InBoard predicates plus NoOverlap, and it is preserved because every slide action checks the very same predicate against the post-state head before committing -- failure to satisfy the guard simply leaves the action disabled.
Saga: The saga pattern is a forward-then-compensate orchestration: when step k fails, the steps committed before it must be unwound in reverse. Two integer counters capture the entire bookkeeping -- done is the number of forward steps that have been committed, undone is the number of compensations still owed -- together with a status enum that distinguishes the four phases of the protocol. Forward advances done while in the forward phase. Fail is the pivot edge: it transitions status to compensating and copies done into undone, which is the moment at which the spec promises that every executed step will be reversed. Compensate decrements undone by one, and Rollback fires only once undone has reached zero, transitioning to rolled_back. The invariant undone <= done holds because undone is initialized to zero in Init, only set to done by Fail, and only decremented by Compensate. The phase-specific clauses (forward implies undone = 0, completed implies done = NumSteps and undone = 0, rolled_back implies undone = 0) close the loop and forbid any state in which forward steps remain pending while compensation has begun.
SelectiveRepeat: Selective-repeat ARQ is interesting because it decouples the per-frame ack from the cumulative slide of the sender's window: the sender retransmits exactly the unacked frames, and the receiver buffers out-of-order arrivals while delivering only the contiguous prefix. The state has a sender side (base, next, acked), a receiver side (rbase, buffer, delivered), and a channel set carrying frames in flight. Window size is enforced by next - base <= W in the type invariant and by next - base < W in SendNew's guard. Retransmit injects an unacked in-window sequence number back into the channel without advancing next, so the sender can recover from a DropFrame loss. RecvFrame buffers any in-window arrival and marks acked, but it does not deliver -- delivery is reserved for DeliverNext, which advances rbase only when buffer[rbase] is set. SlideBase consumes the fully-acked prefix on the sender side. The in-order-delivery invariant is the equation Len(delivered) = rbase combined with delivered[i] = i - 1, which holds because every Append immediately bumps rbase and stores the next sequential id.
SerializableSnapshot: Serializable snapshot isolation builds on plain snapshot isolation by tracking read-write antidependencies and aborting any transaction that would close a serialization cycle. The state has three pieces per transaction: its current phase (running, committed, aborted) and two adjacency sets rwOut and rwIn that record the antidependency graph edges. RWConflict adds an edge from a reader to a concurrent writer, but only between two running transactions, so committed transactions become structurally immutable in the graph. The interesting decision happens at commit time: CommitOk fires only when the transaction is *not* a pivot -- that is, when it does not have both incoming and outgoing rw edges. AbortPivot is the dual edge that fires precisely when the pivot pattern is present. The strong invariant says no committed transaction has both rwIn and rwOut non-empty, which is preserved because the only way to reach committed is through CommitOk, whose guard already enforces this disjunction. Forbidding committed pivots is exactly what rules out two-cycles in the dependency graph and yields serializability.
ShoppingCart: A shopping cart with one-shot checkout has a tiny but interesting state space because three properties must compose: the cart is bounded in size, checkout fires at most once and only after at least one item was added, and the order_count integer is a kind of monotone witness that the checkout edge has actually been taken. The status enum walks shopping -> checked_out or shopping -> abandoned. AddItem and RemoveItem move items within 0..MaxItems, with the cap enforced by the AddItem guard items < MaxItems. Checkout requires items > 0 (you cannot check out an empty cart) and increments order_count to 1 in the same step it transitions to checked_out. The history flag ever_added makes the implication checked_out => ever_added inductive: AddItem is the only edge that raises the flag, and Checkout requires items > 0 which itself implies an earlier AddItem. The clause order_count <= 1 conjoined with (order_count = 1) <=> (status = checked_out) makes the one-shot property a structural equivalence rather than a temporal one.
SkipListAbstract: Modeling a skip list at the abstract level deliberately discards the multilevel index structure and treats the data structure as the sorted set it represents -- the spec is justified because all the safety properties (membership, no-duplicate insert, contains-iff-present) are properties of the underlying set, not of the lookup acceleration. The single state variable elements is a subset of the Universe constant, bounded in cardinality by K. Insert is gated on x not already being in the set and on the cardinality being below K, which gives a precise insertion semantics: idempotent on the second call, capacity-bounded on the K+1th distinct element. Delete simply removes the element if present. Contains is a stuttering observation -- it asserts membership without changing state -- which lets the model exercise queries against arbitrary configurations. The Bounded clause covers both the cardinality fence and the universe-subset constraint, and it is preserved by inspection because Insert is the only growing edge and it explicitly checks the cap before firing.
SleepingBarber: The sleeping-barber problem reduces to a tiny two-variable spec because all customer identity can be abstracted away: only the head count of waiting customers and the barber's binary state matter for safety. The barber scalar lives in {sleeping, cutting} and waiting is an integer in 0..N. The interesting structural invariant is the implication (barber = sleeping) => (waiting = 0), which captures the contract that a sleeping barber means no one is in the queue -- and that holds because the only way to enter sleeping is FinishSleep, which is gated on waiting = 0. WakeBarber is symmetric: it transitions sleeping to cutting only when waiting = 0, so it never bypasses the queue discipline. JoinQueue is gated on barber = cutting and waiting < N, the latter being the chair-capacity bound that keeps waiting in 0..N. FinishWithNext decrements waiting while leaving barber in cutting, modeling the immediate take-next behavior. The customers-leave-when-full case is modeled by the *absence* of a transition, which is the cleanest way to express it without introducing a sentinel.
PegSolitaire: The interesting modeling question for peg solitaire is what to count, not what to move. A board function from cells to {0,1} captures the geometry, but the property of interest -- that every legal jump removes exactly one peg -- is a counting argument, so an explicit moves variable sits alongside the board. With that in hand the inductive invariant is the elegant equation PegCount + moves = N - 1, which is established at Init (six pegs, zero moves) and preserved by Jump because the EXCEPT updates flip exactly one occupied cell to empty (the jumped peg q) and swap the source p with the destination e, leaving the population down by one. Collinearity is enforced by the disjunction (q = p+1 /\ e = p+2) or its mirror, which is sufficient for a one-row board and sidesteps any need for a separate adjacency relation. Done is the natural deadlock predicate -- no triple of cells satisfies the jump pattern -- and it makes the spec stutter cleanly once the puzzle is stuck.
